charts.title: Week 8 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics in Week 8.

nodes.label:
Week 8
Fair Division: Indivisible Goods
nodes.note: Week 8 covers fair division when goods cannot be split. Exact fairness (envy-freeness, proportionality) is generally infeasible, so relaxed notions EF1 and PROP1 are studied. Round-Robin and Envy-Cycle Elimination both achieve EF1.

nodes.label:
Indivisible Goods
Model
nodes.note: An allocation problem has m indivisible goods and n agents with valuation functions. Additive valuations satisfy v(S) = sum of individual good values. The goal is a complete allocation (partition of goods) satisfying fairness criteria.

nodes.label:
Exact Fairness:
EF and Proportionality
nodes.note: Envy-freeness (no agent prefers another's bundle) and proportionality (each gets 1/n of total value) are generally infeasible for indivisible goods. Example: 2 agents, 1 item — one agent must get nothing.

nodes.label:
Relaxed Fairness:
EF1 and PROP1
nodes.note: EF1 (envy-free up to 1 good): removing some item from the envied bundle eliminates envy. PROP1 (proportional up to 1 good): adding one item reaches proportional share. For additive valuations, EF1 implies PROP1.

nodes.label:
Round-Robin
Algorithm
nodes.note: Agents take turns picking their favourite remaining item in a fixed order. Round-Robin achieves EF1 (and hence PROP1) for additive valuations in polynomial time. Not Pareto-optimal and not strategyproof.

nodes.label:
Envy-Cycle Elimination
(ECE)
nodes.note: ECE works for monotone valuations (not just additive). It iteratively allocates items to agents with no incoming envy edges, using cycle reallocation (RAC) to eliminate cycles. Achieves EF1 in O(m*n^2) time.

nodes.label:
Welfare
Concepts
nodes.note: Three welfare measures: utilitarian (sum), egalitarian (minimum), Nash (product). Maximising utilitarian or Nash welfare yields Pareto-optimal allocations. Nash welfare maximiser additionally achieves EF1 for additive valuations.

nodes.label:
EFX: Envy-Free
Up to Any Good
nodes.note: EFX strengthens EF1: removing ANY item from the envied bundle eliminates envy. EFX always exists for 2 or 3 agents with additive valuations. For 4+ agents it is an open problem — one of the most important unsolved questions in fair division.

nodes.label:
Maximin Share
(MMS) Guarantee
nodes.note: The MMS of agent i is the best value i can guarantee if they cut the goods into n piles knowing they get the worst pile. MMS allocations always exist for n=2 but may not for n>=3. However, 3/4 of MMS can always be guaranteed.

nodes.label:
Envy Graph
& RAC
nodes.note: The envy graph has an edge from i to j if i envies j. Reallocation along a cycle (RAC) rotates bundles around a cycle in the envy graph; the set of bundles doesn't change but all agents on the cycle improve.

nodes.label:
Nash Welfare:
PO + EF1
nodes.note: The allocation maximising the product of utilities (Nash welfare) is always Pareto-optimal and EF1 for additive valuations. This makes it a particularly attractive objective combining fairness and efficiency.

charts.title: Round-Robin Algorithm
charts.description: Agents take turns picking favourite items; guarantees EF1 and PROP1 for additive valuations.

nodes.label:
Start: n agents, m goods,
additive valuations
nodes.note: We have n agents with additive valuations and m indivisible goods to allocate. We will use Round-Robin to guarantee EF1.

nodes.label:
Fix ordering of agents;
all goods unallocated
nodes.note: Choose a fixed picking order (e.g., 1, 2, ..., n, 1, 2, ...). This order is repeated until all goods are allocated.

nodes.label:
Any goods
remaining?
nodes.note: Check if there are still unallocated goods. If yes, the next agent in the picking order takes their turn. If no goods remain, the allocation is complete.

nodes.label:
Next agent picks
their favourite remaining good
nodes.note: The current agent selects the unallocated good with the highest value to them (their most preferred remaining item) and adds it to their bundle.

nodes.label:
EF1 allocation
complete
nodes.note: Round-Robin has produced an EF1 (and PROP1 for additive valuations) complete allocation. Any earlier picker does not envy later pickers; any later picker does not envy the earlier picker after removing the earlier picker's first item.

charts.title: Envy-Cycle Elimination (ECE) Algorithm
charts.description: Achieves EF1 for monotone valuations by iteratively allocating to unenvied agents and eliminating envy cycles.

nodes.label:
Start: n agents,
monotone valuations, empty bundles
nodes.note: Initialise with empty bundles. An empty allocation is trivially EF1. We will maintain EF1 as we allocate items one by one.

nodes.label:
Any items
unallocated?
nodes.note: If all items have been allocated, the algorithm terminates with an EF1 allocation. Otherwise, we need to allocate the next item while maintaining EF1.

nodes.label:
Does the envy graph
contain a cycle?
nodes.note: The envy graph has an edge from i to j if agent i envies agent j. In any finite graph where every node has at most one outgoing edge, there is either a cycle or a source (unenvied node).

nodes.label:
Perform RAC:
rotate bundles around cycle
nodes.note: Reallocation along the cycle gives each agent on the cycle the bundle of the next agent. Bundle sets don't change, so EF1 is preserved. The cycle is eliminated (at least one envy edge removed).

nodes.label:
Find agent with
no incoming envy edges
nodes.note: After cycle elimination, there must be a source node (agent no one envies) in the envy graph. Giving this agent an item cannot make others envy them (by monotonicity).

nodes.label:
Give unenvied agent
one unallocated item
nodes.note: Allocate any unallocated item to the unenvied agent. By monotonicity, adding an item to their bundle can only increase their value, preserving EF1 for all pairs.

nodes.label:
EF1 allocation
complete
nodes.note: All items allocated. The result is EF1 for monotone valuations. Total runtime is O(m * n^2): at most m item allocations, with up to n(n-1) RAC steps between each.

charts.title: Fairness Concepts: Strength Hierarchy
charts.description: Classification of fairness concepts from strongest to weakest for indivisible goods.

nodes.label:
Fairness Concepts
for Indivisible Goods
nodes.note: Multiple fairness concepts exist for indivisible goods, ranging from the ideal (exact envy-freeness) to always-achievable relaxations. Stronger concepts give better guarantees but are harder or impossible to achieve.

nodes.label:
EF: Envy-Free
(exact, rarely achievable)
nodes.note: No agent prefers any other agent's bundle. Generally infeasible for indivisible goods: with 2 agents and 1 item, at least one agent is envious. The gold standard but rarely achievable.

nodes.label:
EFX: EF up to any
good (open problem for n>=4)
nodes.note: Removing any single item from the envied bundle eliminates envy. Stronger than EF1. Exists for n=2 (monotone) and n=3 (additive). Whether it exists for n>=4 with additive valuations is unsolved.

nodes.label:
EF1: EF up to
one good (always achievable)
nodes.note: There exists some item in the envied bundle whose removal eliminates envy. Always achievable for additive (Round-Robin) and monotone (ECE) valuations in polynomial time.

nodes.label:
PROP: Proportional
(rarely achievable)
nodes.note: Each agent values their bundle at at least 1/n of the total. Generally infeasible for indivisible goods, just like exact envy-freeness.

nodes.label:
PROP1: Proportional
up to 1 good
nodes.note: Adding one item to an agent's bundle reaches their proportional share. For additive valuations, EF1 plus completeness implies PROP1.

nodes.label:
MMS: Maximin Share
(not always achievable for n>=3)
nodes.note: Each agent values their bundle at least as much as the value they would guarantee if they cut the goods into n piles and got the worst pile. Always achievable for n=2; counterexamples exist for n>=3 but 3/4 of MMS is always achievable.
